text: The rise of AI agents is pushing the boundaries of AI infrastructure. With the autonomous nature of these agents, they require continuous execution. Think about thousands of agents that require massive throughput in AI accelerators, which is the foundational enabler for continuous real-time autonomy for complex AI agents. Additionally, because these AI agents are operating on long horizon tasks, it becomes critical to enable vast context windows. These are critical memory workings that grounds AI agents, enabling deep situational awareness and the execution of complex long horizon tasks. Last but not least, there is a real-time reasoning component to AI agents. They require AI infrastructure that provides high-speed inference and low latency memory access that is necessary for continuous real-time agentic orchestration. These demands, we're seeing them across the entire model development life cycle of the agentic era. Starting with pre-training the mixture of experts models that drive these AI agents. Models are rising up to the 10 trillion parameter sizes, and this requires optimized hardware that is specialized for high throughput pre-training. And even before you launch the model for serving, we're seeing the rise of reinforcement learning techniques like sampling, where the model goes through several iterations of reasoning loops to make it more efficient and more accurate. And then you serve hundreds of thousands of AI agents that are continuously prompting and waiting for responses, requiring efficiency in latency. With these demands across this entire AI agentic development life cycle, we're seeing that the bottleneck for innovation has shifted from just being algorithmic design efficiency to being both algorithmic design and the underlying AI infrastructure. That's why today, I would like to talk about architecting infrastructure for the AI native future, specifically focusing on how you can scale autonomous agents on Google's TPUs. When you think about specialized hardware for AI ML workloads, you've got CPUs that are generalist and serial thinkers. So, while they're versatile, they're not very specialized for one specific tasks. They're very flexible, but they're bottlenecked by high throughput tasks. And then on the other hand, you've got GPUs. They're also generalist, but they're parallel thinkers. They excel at parallel tasks, like graphics and AI ML tasks. They use massive parallelism to prevent any throughput bottlenecks. Now, TPUs on the other hand, they're specialist. They're specifically specialized for the mathematics that forms and powers the foundation of AI models, specifically matrix multiplications. TPUs are ideal for high-performance machine learning workloads. At Google, we've been designing and engineering Tensor Processing Units for over a decade to specifically break through the arithmetic intensity and the interconnect walls that severely restrict agentic systems. Over the past 10 years, we've been innovating across the entire TPU road map since we launched our first TPU v1 in 2015. We've added technologies like distributed shared memory across ICI to efficient cooling with liquid cooling to introduction of 3D Taurus architecture and topologies so that we build massive supercomputers that all work together coherently. But what I'm really excited about is some of the technologies that we have added to TPU 8T and TPU 8I, which is the newly announced and newly launched TPUs. So for the entirety of my talk today, I'll be focusing some of those key technologies and advancements that we have added to TPU 8T and TPU 8I. Now reimagining the accelerator architecture starts from the specialized TPU silicon features themselves, extends to how you innovate around the ICI interchip interconnect topologies, all the way to how you scale out data center networking capabilities. However, the story doesn't end there. You really need to think about how you deliver power to this TPU chips efficiently, and with the TPUs is vertical power delivery, and how you efficiently cool the systems with technologies like liquid cool cooling on TPUs. Now let's zoom in and start with TPU 8T, which is the throughput optimized silicon. With TPU 8T, you have a tensor core that has a bunch of MXUs, AXUs, and VPUs, which are computational units that are optimized for matrix multiplication that form the foundation of AI computation. We support low precision arithmetic from FP4, FP8, BF16, and also support for mixed precision optimizations as well. One of the things that we've done to TPU 8T is move things like block scale multiplication inside of the MXUs from the VPUs. This allows for zero VPU overhead for quantized matmuls, and allows for better model accuracy and better model flop utilization. Another technology for TPUs is systolic arrays. Rather than going back and forth between memory and the computational units, you actually open up pipelines that enable the constant flow of data from the memory into the computational units, which ensures that this MXUs constantly are fed with data and continue computation and are not waiting on data. Additionally, we interconnect all these chips to function as single unit in an ICA domain using a proprietary ICI interchip interconnect technology. Another key technology for TPUs is sparse core. These are specialized cores that we've added onto the silicon. And prior to TPU AT, you could utilize sparse cores to accelerate embeddings heavy workloads by essentially overlapping the dense layer onto the as it is computationally heavy with the embeddings layer offloaded onto the sparse core. You could accelerate embeddings heavy workloads. In TPU AT, we've gone a step further and added what we call an LLM decoder engine. An LLM decoder engine enables sparse cores to have the right amount of arithmetic intensity such that we can utilize the same technique we're doing of on embeddings models, utilize that on LLMs when it comes to overlapping prefill on the tensor cores and decode on the sparse cores that are supported by the LLM decoder engine. When it comes to connectivity between the TPUs, you have to think about how you're transferring data between TPUs that are coherently working together as a single unit. That's where we introduce TPU RDMA and TPU direct storage. With TPU RDMA, you can directly transfer data between two TPU HBMs via the network interface cards, essentially eliminating hops via the host memory. This improves data transfer latency drastically. You can even extend that technology to TPU direct storage, where essentially you can copy data directly from managed storage onto the HBM of the TPU chips. This really works very, very well for long context KV cache storing onto managed storage, but yet utilizing that in the TPU chips. Because now you can directly access those long context KV cache in managed storage by copying the data from managed storage onto directly onto the TPU HBM. And then when you go beyond data transfer, and when you think about TPU chips working coherently together, you have to think about the scale-up technology for these TPU accelerators. That's where TPUs excel with the throughput-optimized 3D torus ICI domain technology. With 3D torus topology, every single TPU chip is connected to its nearest neighbor with wrap-around links that form a cube of 64 TPU chips in this 3D torus topology. Think of a cube of TPU chips being a single rack that is actually sitting on the data center floor. We then connect all of the single racks with optical circuit switching using optical fibers. These interconnected racks form a TPU supercomputer of 9,600 TPU H chips. This deliver 121 exaflops of FP4 computes with over 2 petabytes of shared HBM. And over 19 terabits per second bidirectional ICI bandwidth. And remember I talked about optical circuit switching. This is such a unique and really cool technology that we have added to TPUs. So essentially, input fiber that is coming from one cube can be redirected without any human interaction or automatically in software. That input can be redirected to either cube Z or cube Y's output fiber without any human interaction. All happening within the software. This eliminates any bandwidth bottlenecks and delivers 30% lower cost and 40% lower power needs. With the topology flexibility and diversity OCS delivers, we can effectively support model and data parallelism, which is key to accelerating AI workloads. The story doesn't end with scale up. It also extends to scale out. And you have to innovate in how you scale out AI accelerators into hundreds of thousands of accelerators all working together on a single workload. With that, we're introducing Virgo Net architecture to TPU AT. With Virgo networking, a single fabric scales to the entire data center building. Virgo Net architecture allows for the connectivity of over 134,000 TPU chips in a non-blocking single fabric. And if you connect these fabrics using distributed global WAN, you can create training clusters of over 1 million TPUs across all of these fabrics. This delivers a 40% lower latency when it comes to distributed large-scale training. Now, the real hero of the agentic era is lower latency inference. If you think about latency, it is transitioned from just being a user experience or just another metric into being really a cognitive constraint. In agentic workflows, latency it's not just a user experience metric. It is the foundational boundary that essentially detects how efficient agents can operate and can serve. Coupled with that, there's the cost iteration. Agentic use cases often require multiple internal reasoning loops to make the agents more accurate and perform the task more accurately in real life. And last but not least, an agent is only as reliable as the consistency of the underlying model execution and the infrastructure that is enabling it. That's why we're introducing TPU AI, which is the latency-optimized silicon. Now, in addition to all the other good stuff I've already mentioned in the TPU 8i, we've done some amazing stuff to TPU 8i to make it highly optimized for lower latency inference. Starting with a very large SRAM we've added. With a very large SRAM, you can keep constantly fetch data very close to the actual computational units. For example, KV cache. With a large KV cache that is stored right next to the computational units, you essentially reduce the overhead of having to go fetch this KV cache from memory. We've also doubled the interchip interconnect bandwidth and the speed for chip-to-chip communication. In addition to that, we have also added a specialized Pascal-based collective acceleration engine that will deliver an additional 5x reduction in latency for collective communication. When you really think about low latency inference, you have to be efficient in how you moving data from the memory onto the computational units. In a typical GPU floor, you have data moving from the L2 cache to the L1 to the tensor cores where operations are happening on the small tiles. But on the other hand with TPUs, large inputs are copied to the shared vector memory. And these large inputs are further streamlined to the matrix multiplication units. And this allows for constantly feeding these computational units with data and allows for high model flop utilization and constant and keeps these MXUs constantly busy doing computation, which is very, very efficient. Even when it comes to the topology itself of interconnecting these TPU chips, you have to innovate. That's why we introduced the board fly topology to TPU 8 i. This is a non-torus topology that interconnects up to 1,152 TPU 8 i chips. The way to think about board fly topology is that it is hierarchical in nature. It starts with four fully connected TPU chips on each TPU board and eight of those boards are fully connected into a group, which is essentially the rack on the data center floor. And 36 of those groups are then also connected via OCS to form this pod of 1,152 TPU chips. This particular topology enables a 53% reduction in network diameter between any two TPU chips within the 1,152 TPU port. This further enables a over 50% improvement in latency. Now at Google, we're always thinking about the next frontier of AI models. And we believe that the frontier is going to be low latency inference. We're already seeing the raw LLM AI agents are playing in AI advancement. We believe that LLM agents will continue to require more inference cycles to perform this deeper reasoning per second. But in addition to that, we see this rise of world models. These are models that can essentially in real time create simulated environments of the real workings of the real world and allows for interactivity at a frame-by-frame basis. What this means is that you could actually take AI agents and have them operate and practice operating in this simulated real-time environment before you actually have them perform tasks in the real world where they're now more trained and more accurate. And then on the other hand, we see the rise of physical AI, specifically robotics and autonomous systems that require reaction in the milliseconds regime, which makes low latency inference key. And even with the intersection of physical AI and world models, where we're seeing robots and autonomous systems first practice how to act in the real world, essentially practice that in real-time simulated environments developed by world models. We're super excited at Google in terms of the trends of AI development and where AI is going, and we're super excited to continue innovating our AI infrastructure and TPUs to meet these demands and needs for this frontier AI. Be sure to check out more information on the TPU website, and thank you.
